slides.title: We Know The Why — Performance & Compliance Audit
slides.content:
Client: WKTW (weknowthewhy.com)
Auditor: w.k.t.w.
Audit Scope: Full-site technical audit across Server, Platform/CMS, and Template tiers
Pages Crawled: 9 (homepage, /about/, /contact/, /proof/, legal pages, article pages)
Audit Date: [Insert Date]

Scope Summary:
• 92 total findings across 3 tiers
• Server: 9 findings | Platform/CMS: 17 findings | Template: 66 findings
• 16 findings carry legal liability designation
• 10 systemic root cause clusters identified
• 42 systemic findings | 38 isolated findings
slides.notes: Opening slide. Sets professional context. Emphasize that this is a structured, evidence-based audit — not a subjective review. The 3-tier framework (Server / Platform / Template) maps directly to who owns the fix: hosting/DevOps, the Astro/Netlify platform configuration, or the page template authors. Finding IDs referenced throughout: full inventory available in the technical appendix. The 9-page crawl covers all primary conversion paths and content types.

slides.title: Executive Summary
slides.content:
Overall Site Health: MODERATE RISK

What's Working Well:
✓ Exceptional server performance — TTFB 121ms, HTTP/2 + TLS 1.3
✓ Zero layout shift (CLS 0.000) — visually stable on all tested pages
✓ Excellent interaction responsiveness — INP 24ms (top cohort)
✓ Minimal JavaScript footprint — 29KB total, single third-party script
✓ Clean DOM architecture — semantic HTML, no ghost markup
✓ Font loading correctly configured — self-hosted, font-display: swap

Where the Site Is Exposed:
⚠ CRITICAL: The site cannot measure its own performance — zero conversion tracking on the primary KPI (contact form)
⚠ CRITICAL: Consent infrastructure exists in code but never renders — users cannot grant or deny consent
⚠ HIGH: 16 findings carry legal liability (WCAG/ADA + GDPR/CCPA)
⚠ HIGH: Every page requires a visitor to self-navigate to /contact/ — no inline conversion path exists anywhere
⚠ MEDIUM: Static assets force full revalidation on every visit — repeat visitor performance is unnecessarily degraded

The Core Tension:
The site's technical foundation is genuinely strong. The risks are concentrated in three areas: legal compliance, conversion architecture, and measurement. These are fixable — and fixing them does not require rebuilding the site.
slides.notes: This slide is the executive anchor. Lead with the positives — they are real and significant. A 121ms TTFB and 24ms INP on a production site is genuinely excellent. Then pivot: the performance foundation is solid, but it is currently invisible to the business (no conversion tracking) and legally exposed (consent + WCAG). The 'core tension' framing is important — this is not a broken site, it is a well-built site with specific, addressable gaps. Avoid any language suggesting the site is fundamentally flawed. Key finding IDs for this summary: [server-1-ttfb-pass], [visual-stability-cls-zero-pass], [inp-interaction-excellent], [js-minimal-payload-pass], [analytics-no-ga4-no-conversion-tracking], [privacy-consent-banner-absent], [ux-conversion-no-form-on-page], [server-4-cache-control-revalidate-every-request].

slides.title: Findings at a Glance
slides.content:
Finding Distribution by Tier:
┌─────────────────┬────────┬──────────────┐
│ Tier            │ Count  │ Systemic     │
├─────────────────┼────────┼──────────────┤
│ Template        │  66    │ 38 systemic  │
│ Platform/CMS    │  17    │  4 systemic  │
│ Server          │   9    │  0 systemic  │
└─────────────────┴────────┴──────────────┘

Finding Distribution by Severity:
• Critical: 1 (analytics blind spot)
• High: 8 (touch targets, form labels, CSP, conversion paths)
• Medium: 38 (consent, UX, caching, structured data, fonts)
• Low: 45 (passes, minor optimizations, informational)

Legal Liability Findings: 16
• WCAG/ADA exposure: 12 findings
• GDPR/CCPA exposure: 6 findings
• (Some findings carry dual exposure)

Key Insight: 42 of 92 findings are systemic — they stem from shared architectural decisions, not one-off mistakes. Fixing the root cause resolves multiple findings simultaneously.
slides.notes: This is a navigation slide — it orients the audience before diving into root causes. The key message is that the finding count (92) is less important than the pattern count (10 root cause clusters). Emphasize that 42 systemic findings means the remediation effort is more efficient than the raw number suggests — fixing one architectural decision can close 5-8 findings at once. The 45 low-severity findings include many PASSes and informational items — this is a sign of audit thoroughness, not site failure. Do not dwell on the low-severity count. Pivot quickly to the root cause clusters on the next slide.

slides.title: Legal & Compliance Exposure — Executive Risk Assessment
slides.content:
16 Findings Carry Legal Liability Designation

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
GDPR / CCPA EXPOSURE (6 findings)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

① Consent Banner Never Renders
The consent infrastructure exists in the codebase (buttons, Google Consent Mode v2 defaults) but the banner never becomes visible to users. Users cannot grant or deny consent. Google Consent Mode is permanently locked to 'denied' for 100% of visitors.
Regulatory mechanism: GDPR Article 7 requires freely given, informed consent via an affirmative act. A banner that never appears cannot satisfy this requirement.

② Analytics Fires Before Consent Is Possible
Plausible Analytics loads at 161ms — before any consent interaction can occur. Even if the banner were visible, analytics would have already fired.
Regulatory mechanism: The ePrivacy Directive governs script-based tracking independent of cookie use. Cookieless analytics tools are not automatically exempt.

③ Content Security Policy Absent
No CSP header is present on any page. CSP is the browser-enforced mechanism that prevents unauthorized scripts from executing — including scripts that could exfiltrate form data.
Regulatory mechanism: GDPR Article 32 requires appropriate technical measures to ensure data security. Absence of CSP on a site with a contact form is a documented gap in this obligation.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
WCAG / ADA EXPOSURE (12 findings)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

④ Contrast Failures Across All 9 Pages
Navigation links at 1.06:1 contrast ratio. Metadata spans at 1.62:1. The consent accept button at 1.00:1 (invisible text on identical background). WCAG minimum is 4.5:1.
Legal mechanism: ADA Title III web accessibility lawsuits are well-documented and frequently cite contrast failures as primary violations.

⑤ Touch Targets Below Minimum on Every Page
16+ interactive elements below the 48×48px WCAG minimum — including navigation links, footer links, and the consent banner's own action buttons. The privacy policy link within the consent context measures 91×17px.
Legal mechanism: WCAG 2.5.8 (AA) failures are an increasingly common basis for ADA demand letters.

⑥ Contact Form Has an Unlabeled Field
One form field on the site's sole conversion page lacks a programmatic label. Screen reader users cannot identify the field.
Legal mechanism: WCAG 4.1.2 (Name, Role, Value) is a Level A criterion — the baseline conformance threshold. Level A failures are the most commonly cited in ADA complaints.

⑦ Footer Button Has No Accessible Name
A button in the footer (likely a consent re-trigger) has no visible label and no aria-label. Its purpose is unknown to sighted and assistive technology users alike.

⑧ Heading Structure Skips Levels on /contact/
The contact page jumps from h1 to h3, breaking screen reader heading navigation on the site's primary conversion page.

Compound Risk Note: The consent banner concentrates multiple violations simultaneously — invisible text (1.00:1 contrast), undersized touch targets (17px privacy link), missing accessible name (footer button), and non-functional consent flow. A single component carries GDPR, CCPA, and WCAG exposure concurrently.
slides.notes: This slide must land with weight. The audience needs to understand that these are not theoretical risks — they are documented regulatory mechanisms with real enforcement histories. Do NOT cite specific fine amounts, settlement ranges, or percentage penalties — these vary by jurisdiction, enforcement history, and company size, and citing them without client-specific data would be misleading. Instead, explain the mechanism: why the regulation applies, what the violation is, and what the consequence pathway looks like. The compound risk note at the bottom is the most important insight on this slide — one broken component (the consent banner) simultaneously creates GDPR, CCPA, and WCAG exposure. That is the story. Finding IDs: [privacy-consent-banner-absent], [privacy-2-pre-consent-plausible-fires], [prescan-escalation-csp-confirmed-absent], [escalation-resolution-csp], [det-wcag-insufficient-contrast-ratio-https-weknowthewhy-com], [a11y-2-touch-targets-undersized], [touch-targets-ux-impact], [mobile-privacy-policy-link-critically-undersized], [a11y-1-form-missing-label], [form-1-missing-label], [interactive-footer-button-purpose-unclear], [det-wcag-improper-content-structure-https-weknowthewhy-com-contact], [det-wcag-missing-name-role-value-https-weknowthewhy-com-contact], [det-security-headers-https-weknowthewhy-com-about].

slides.title: Root Cause Cluster 1 — The Broken Consent System
slides.content:
8 Findings | GDPR + CCPA + WCAG Exposure | Systemic

The Problem in Plain Language:
The site has a consent system — it just doesn't work. The consent banner exists in the HTML, the accept/decline buttons exist, and Google Consent Mode v2 is configured with 'denied' defaults. But the banner never becomes visible to users. The result is a system that signals intent to collect data while providing no mechanism for users to authorize it.

What This Means in Practice:
• Every visitor arrives on a site where consent infrastructure is present but non-functional
• Google Consent Mode is permanently 'denied' — consent-gated analytics can never activate
• The consent accept button has a 1.00:1 contrast ratio — identical foreground and background colors — making it invisible even if the banner did render
• The privacy policy link within the consent context is 91×17px — 35% of the WCAG minimum touch target size
• A footer button (likely the consent re-trigger) has no accessible name — its purpose is unknown
• Plausible Analytics fires at 161ms, before any consent interaction is possible

Root Cause:
A custom consent implementation with a rendering bug or an unmet initialization condition. The banner is architecturally present but never reaches the user's screen.

Findings in This Cluster:
[privacy-consent-banner-absent] [consent-banner-not-rendering] [privacy-2-pre-consent-plausible-fires] [ghost-2-consent-banner-hidden-interactive] [mobile-privacy-policy-link-critically-undersized] [interactive-footer-button-purpose-unclear] [ux-mobile-consent-banner-viewport-impact] [prescan-escalation-console-errors]

Business Impact Mechanism:
This cluster simultaneously blocks analytics data collection (consent never granted → consent-gated tracking never activates), creates regulatory exposure (no functional consent mechanism), and excludes users from exercising data rights (no visible accept/decline). Fixing the rendering bug is the single highest-leverage action in the entire audit — it resolves 8 findings and unblocks the analytics infrastructure.
slides.notes: This is the most urgent cluster. The framing should be: 'You have a consent system. It doesn't work. Here is what that means.' Avoid technical jargon about rendering bugs — focus on the consequence chain. The 1.00:1 contrast ratio on the accept button is a striking detail — use it. An accept button with invisible text is not a functional consent mechanism by any definition. The business impact mechanism is important: this is not just a compliance issue. A non-functional consent system means Google Consent Mode is permanently denied, which means consent-gated analytics (GA4, Google Ads conversion tracking) can never activate even if those tools are added later. The consent system is the prerequisite for the analytics infrastructure. Fix it first.

slides.title: Root Cause Cluster 2 — The Measurement Blind Spot
slides.content:
3 Findings | Critical Severity | Systemic

The Problem in Plain Language:
The business cannot measure whether its website is working. The contact form — the stated primary KPI — has no submission tracking. There is no way to count conversions, attribute them to traffic sources, or evaluate whether any marketing or content investment is producing results.

The Current Analytics Stack:
• Plausible Analytics: client-side only, no custom events configured, blockable by ad blockers
• No GA4, no GTM, no data layer, no server-side fallback
• Contact form submissions: untracked
• CTA clicks: untracked
• Traffic source attribution to conversions: impossible

Why Client-Side-Only Is a Structural Risk for This Audience:
The B2B technical audience that WKTW targets has disproportionately high ad blocker adoption. A client-side-only analytics script loaded from a third-party domain (plausible.io) is silently blocked for a meaningful portion of this audience. The site has no way to know how large that portion is — because the measurement tool itself is what gets blocked.

The Consequence:
The business is making decisions about content, marketing, and conversion optimization without a reliable numerator (form completions) or denominator (total sessions). The conversion rate — the primary KPI — is currently unmeasurable.

Findings in This Cluster:
[analytics-no-ga4-no-conversion-tracking] [ux-analytics-client-side-only-data-loss] [analytics-plausible-single-point-failure-conversion-blindspot]

Business Impact Mechanism:
Without conversion tracking, every optimization decision is made without feedback. Content changes, CTA experiments, and marketing spend cannot be evaluated for effectiveness. The contact form KPI exists as a business goal but not as a measurable data point.
slides.notes: This cluster is the most directly business-critical finding in the audit. The framing is not 'your analytics are bad' — it is 'you cannot measure your primary KPI.' That is a strategic problem, not a technical one. The ad blocker point is important context for this specific audience: B2B technical buyers are the heaviest ad blocker users. A consultancy that audits websites for a living is likely to have prospects who block third-party analytics scripts. The fix is a three-layer redundant measurement stack: proxied client-side Plausible (survives ad blockers), GTM-managed dataLayer events (structured event taxonomy), and a server-side form submission signal (survives all client-side blocking). Finding IDs: [analytics-no-ga4-no-conversion-tracking], [ux-analytics-client-side-only-data-loss], [analytics-plausible-single-point-failure-conversion-blindspot].

slides.title: Root Cause Cluster 3 — The Conversion Architecture Gap
slides.content:
9 Findings | High Severity | Systemic

The Problem in Plain Language:
Every page on the site terminates without a conversion mechanism. The contact form exists only on /contact/. Every other page — service pages, the About page, the Proof page, legal pages, article pages — ends without an inline path to act. Visitors must independently decide to navigate to /contact/ to convert.

The Current Conversion Path:
Visitor arrives → reads content → reaches end of page → no CTA → must self-navigate to /contact/ → form

What Is Missing:
• No above-the-fold CTA on the homepage
• No inline form on service pages, proof page, or article pages
• No sticky navigation CTA on mobile
• No contextual prompt at the end of persuasive content
• Trust signals (testimonials, client logos, case study results) absent from all pages including /proof/
• The article about audit failures — the most topically relevant content for the firm's service — does not link to the service page
• No pricing signals, engagement model indicators, or scope context — visitors cannot self-qualify before contacting

Root Cause:
This is a content architecture decision, not a template bug. The site was built as a brand presence, not a conversion engine. The conversion path was not designed into the information architecture.

Findings in This Cluster:
[ux-conversion-no-form-on-page] [ux-conversion-01] [revenue-no-service-page-link-in-article] [ux-conversion-cta-clarity-competing-actions] [trust-signals-absent-at-conversion-points] [revenue-no-social-proof-above-fold] [ux-revenue-no-pricing-signals] [ux-conversion-01 (CTA below fold)] [prescan-override-1-contact-form-severity]

Business Impact Mechanism:
The mechanism is direct: a visitor who completes a service page, finishes reading a proof case, or reaches the end of a relevant article has no on-page action available. The only options are back-navigation or tab close. Every page that terminates without a conversion mechanism is a potential exit point for a visitor who had intent but no affordance to act on it.
slides.notes: This cluster tells the most important strategic story in the audit. The site is well-written and well-built — but it is not designed to convert. The framing should be: 'The site is doing the persuasion work. It is not doing the conversion work.' The article-to-service-page finding is a particularly concrete example: a visitor who finds the audit failures article via search, reads it, and is persuaded — then has nowhere to go. The article does not link to the service. That is a missed conversion at the moment of maximum intent. The trust signals finding is also important for a B2B consultancy: the /proof/ page exists but contains no testimonials, client logos, or case study results. Social proof is the primary conversion mechanism for professional services. Finding IDs: [ux-conversion-no-form-on-page], [ux-conversion-01], [revenue-no-service-page-link-in-article], [ux-conversion-cta-clarity-competing-actions], [trust-signals-absent-at-conversion-points], [revenue-no-social-proof-above-fold], [ux-revenue-no-pricing-signals].

slides.title: Root Cause Cluster 4 — The Design System Contrast Failure
slides.content:
2 Findings | WCAG/ADA Legal Liability | Systemic Across All 9 Pages

The Problem in Plain Language:
The site's color palette uses foreground and background colors that are too close in luminance. The same contrast failures appear on every audited page — this is not a per-page mistake, it is a design system problem. The color tokens for certain UI states were defined without contrast verification.

The Specific Failures:
• Navigation links: 1.06:1 contrast ratio (WCAG minimum: 4.5:1)
• Metadata spans: 1.62:1 contrast ratio
• Consent accept button: 1.00:1 contrast ratio (identical foreground and background — text is invisible)
• Inline links: simultaneously fail WCAG 1.4.3 (contrast) AND 1.4.1 (color as sole distinguishing indicator)

Why This Is a Design System Issue, Not a Page Issue:
The same CSS selectors fail across all 9 crawled pages. A single change to the color token definitions in the base CSS or Astro layout component propagates the fix to every page simultaneously.

The Compound Failure:
Inline links fail two WCAG criteria at once: they are not sufficiently contrasted from the background (1.4.3), AND they are distinguished from body text only by color — with no underline or other non-color indicator (1.4.1). Fixing only one criterion leaves the compound failure partially intact.

Findings in This Cluster:
[det-wcag-insufficient-contrast-ratio-https-weknowthewhy-com] [det-wcag-color-as-sole-indicator-https-weknowthewhy-com-about] [a11y-compound-contrast-color-indicator-failure]

Business Impact Mechanism:
Low-vision users who rely on screen magnification or high-contrast modes may be unable to identify inline links as interactive targets. On a site where inline links are the primary navigation mechanism within content, this is a direct barrier to content engagement and conversion path navigation — not a theoretical accessibility concern.
slides.notes: The 1.00:1 contrast ratio on the consent accept button is the most striking data point in this cluster — use it as the opening hook. An accept button where the text is literally invisible is a vivid illustration of why design system color tokens need contrast verification. The compound failure framing (1.4.3 + 1.4.1 simultaneously) is important for the legal exposure discussion: these are two independently actionable WCAG violations on the same element. The fix is a single CSS change — but it must address both criteria simultaneously. Finding IDs: [det-wcag-insufficient-contrast-ratio-https-weknowthewhy-com], [det-wcag-color-as-sole-indicator-https-weknowthewhy-com-about], [a11y-compound-contrast-color-indicator-failure].

slides.title: Root Cause Cluster 5 — Touch Target Failures Across All Pages
slides.content:
3 Findings | WCAG 2.5.8 (AA) | Systemic — Every Page Affected

The Problem in Plain Language:
Navigation links, footer links, and contextual links across the site are consistently too small for reliable mobile touch interaction. This is an architectural CSS problem — the site's interactive elements use text-intrinsic sizing without minimum touch target dimensions.

Measured Failures:
• Homepage navigation: 'Home' at 39×20px, 'The Get Right' at 90×20px
• Footer links: 'Terms' at 41×48px (width acceptable, height borderline), privacy policy at 91×17px
• Consent banner privacy policy link: 91×17px (35% of WCAG minimum)
• 16+ total undersized touch targets identified across the site

WCAG 2.5.8 Minimum: 24×24px (AA) | Practical Target: 48×48px

Root Cause:
The navigation and footer components were designed for desktop pointer interaction. Mobile-specific padding and min-height rules were not applied. The fix does not require visual redesign — CSS padding and ::before pseudo-element tap-target expanders can expand the interactive surface without altering the visual footprint.

Findings in This Cluster:
[a11y-2-touch-targets-undersized] [touch-targets-ux-impact] [mobile-privacy-policy-link-critically-undersized]

Business Impact Mechanism:
Mis-taps on undersized navigation links cause two failure modes: the user taps an adjacent unintended element (disorienting, breaks trust) or the tap registers on nothing (invisible failure). Both outcomes increase the probability of immediate page exit. Because the header and footer are site-wide components, every mobile session on every page is affected. Google Search Console flags undersized touch targets as a mobile usability issue — this is a measurable search ranking signal.
slides.notes: The 91×17px privacy policy link within the consent context is the most legally significant data point here — it is simultaneously a WCAG violation and a GDPR consent validity issue. A consent mechanism where the privacy policy link is physically inaccessible on mobile undermines the 'informed' requirement of GDPR Article 7. The fix is CSS-only and non-destructive to visual design — this is important to communicate. The business does not need to redesign the site to fix this. Finding IDs: [a11y-2-touch-targets-undersized], [touch-targets-ux-impact], [mobile-privacy-policy-link-critically-undersized], [conv-ux-001-touch-target-form-suppression], [mob-touch-001].

slides.title: Root Cause Cluster 6 — The Contact Form's Structural Vulnerabilities
slides.content:
6 Findings | High Severity | Conversion + Accessibility

The Problem in Plain Language:
The contact form is the site's sole conversion mechanism. It has three structural vulnerabilities that create risk at the moment of highest intent.

Vulnerability 1 — An Unlabeled Field
One form field lacks a programmatic label association. Screen reader users encounter an unidentifiable input. If this field renders as a visible text box (which is possible given the HTML default), sighted users also see a confusing empty field with no label.

Vulnerability 2 — No Error Recovery
The form uses a full-page POST model. If submission fails (network error, server error, validation failure), the browser navigates to an error page and all field values are discarded. A prospect who spent time composing a detailed inquiry loses everything and must start over. There is no evidence of client-side error handling, field state preservation, or retry logic.

Vulnerability 3 — Broken Heading Hierarchy
The contact page jumps from h1 to h3, skipping h2. Screen reader users navigating by heading level encounter a structural gap on the site's most important page.

Findings in This Cluster:
[a11y-1-form-missing-label] [form-1-missing-label] [det-wcag-missing-name-role-value-https-weknowthewhy-com-contact] [det-wcag-improper-content-structure-https-weknowthewhy-com-contact] [conversion-no-error-recovery-evidence] [conv-ux-001-touch-target-form-suppression]

Business Impact Mechanism:
The contact form is the only conversion surface on the site. Vulnerabilities at this point in the funnel have outsized impact: a visitor who has navigated to /contact/, read the page, and begun filling out the form is at maximum intent. An unlabeled field creates confusion. A form that discards input on failure creates abandonment. Both are conversion barriers at the moment when conversion is most likely.
slides.notes: The error recovery finding is the most business-critical item in this cluster. A form that silently discards all input on submission failure is a lead-killing failure mode — and it is invisible in normal testing because developers test on fast, reliable connections. The fix (fetch-based submission with field state preservation and inline error messages) is a well-understood pattern. The unlabeled field is likely a Netlify hidden form-name input — if so, it should be aria-hidden rather than removed, and the fix is a single attribute addition. Verify this during implementation. Finding IDs: [a11y-1-form-missing-label], [form-1-missing-label], [det-wcag-missing-name-role-value-https-weknowthewhy-com-contact], [det-wcag-improper-content-structure-https-weknowthewhy-com-contact], [conversion-no-error-recovery-evidence].

slides.title: Root Cause Cluster 7 — Caching & Compression Not Configured
slides.content:
4 Findings | Medium Severity | Repeat Visitor Performance

The Problem in Plain Language:
Every static asset on the site — CSS files, font files, JavaScript — is served with Cache-Control: public, max-age=0, must-revalidate. This is Netlify's default behavior when no custom cache headers are configured. It means the browser must ask the server 'has this changed?' on every page load, for every asset, on every visit.

What Is Being Revalidated on Every Visit:
• Content-hashed CSS (e.g., Section.iKsGnDKO.css) — the hash in the filename guarantees the content never changes
• Font files (inter-latin.woff2, lora-latin.woff2, jetbrainsmono-latin.woff2) — these are immutable
• JavaScript bundles — content-hashed, immutable

The Correct Configuration:
Content-hashed assets should be served with Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable — telling the browser to cache them for one year without revalidation. The hash in the filename guarantees cache-busting on any content change.

Additional Gap:
Brotli compression was not detected despite Netlify supporting it natively. Gzip is active, but Brotli produces meaningfully smaller output for text assets.

Findings in This Cluster:
[server-4-cache-control-revalidate-every-request] [server-transport-cdn-cache-status-missing] [server-transport-no-brotli] [server-7-dns-prefetch-preconnect-minimal-external]

Business Impact Mechanism:
Repeat visitors — returning prospects, referrals, word-of-mouth traffic — currently experience the same asset download overhead as first-time visitors. For a professional services site where the sales cycle involves multiple visits, this is a measurable performance tax on the highest-value audience segment. The fix is a netlify.toml configuration change — no code changes required.
slides.notes: This cluster is the most purely technical of the root cause groups, but it has a clear business framing: repeat visitors are the highest-value audience for a professional services firm. A prospect who visits the site three times during their evaluation process is downloading the same CSS and font files three times. The fix is a configuration file change — it is one of the lowest-effort, highest-impact items in the entire audit. Emphasize that content-hashed filenames make this safe: the hash guarantees cache-busting on any content change, so there is no risk of users seeing stale content. Finding IDs: [server-4-cache-control-revalidate-every-request], [server-transport-cdn-cache-status-missing], [server-transport-no-brotli], [server-7-dns-prefetch-preconnect-minimal-external].

slides.title: Root Cause Cluster 8 — JetBrains Mono Loads on Every Page
slides.content:
2 Findings | Medium Severity | Page Weight Efficiency

The Problem in Plain Language:
JetBrains Mono — a monospace font designed for code display — is loaded on every page of the site via the global CSS. On the contact page, about page, legal pages, and service pages, this font serves no purpose. No code blocks exist on these pages.

The Numbers:
• JetBrains Mono: 31KB transfer weight
• Total page transfer on lightweight pages: ~106KB
• JetBrains Mono as a percentage of total transfer: ~29%

Root Cause:
Astro's CSS bundling includes all @font-face declarations in the single global stylesheet rather than scoping them to templates that use monospace content. The font is not preloaded (correctly — it is not critical), but it still triggers a network request and font parsing on every page load regardless of whether any monospace content exists.

The Fix:
Move the JetBrains Mono @font-face declaration and font-family assignment into the CodeBlock component's scoped styles. Pages without a CodeBlock component will have neither the CSS declaration nor the font-family assignment — the browser will not fetch the font file.

Findings in This Cluster:
[js-2-jetbrains-mono-font-loaded-unnecessarily] [font-face-overload-3-families] [resource-loading-font-strategy-good]

Business Impact Mechanism:
On pages where JetBrains Mono represents 29% of total transfer weight, eliminating it reduces page weight by approximately that proportion. The effect is most pronounced on constrained mobile connections where every kilobyte competes for bandwidth during the critical loading window. The fix is architectural — it prevents future font additions to global CSS from silently propagating to unrelated page types.
slides.notes: This is a relatively contained finding but it illustrates a broader architectural principle: global CSS should not carry component-specific assets. The 29% of total transfer weight figure is striking for a font that contributes nothing to most pages. The fix is a component-scoping change in Astro — move the @font-face declaration from the global layout to the CodeBlock component. This is a low-effort, high-clarity fix that also establishes the correct pattern for future font additions. Finding IDs: [js-2-jetbrains-mono-font-loaded-unnecessarily], [font-face-overload-3-families], [resource-loading-font-strategy-good].

slides.title: Root Cause Cluster 9 — Structured Data & Social Metadata Gaps
slides.content:
3 Findings | Medium Severity | Search Visibility

The Problem in Plain Language:
The site's structured data and social metadata have gaps that prevent rich search results and degrade social sharing previews. These are not blocking issues — the site is indexed and functional — but they represent missed opportunities for organic visibility.

Specific Gaps:

① BlogPosting Schema Missing Required Properties
Article pages have JSON-LD structured data, but the image property — required for Google article rich results — is likely missing. Pages without a complete image property are ineligible for article rich result cards in search, which display a thumbnail and occupy more visual space than plain blue links.

② Open Graph & Twitter Card Values Unverifiable
OG and Twitter Card meta tags could not be verified from available scan data. Pages missing og:image render as plain text links on LinkedIn, Facebook, Slack, and iMessage — no thumbnail, no rich card.

③ JSON-LD Schema Has Unknown Type and Unresolvable Properties
The site's JSON-LD includes an unrecognized @type and relative (rather than absolute) URLs on properties like logo. Google silently discards JSON-LD blocks with unrecognized types — the structured data investment produces no search benefit.

Findings in This Cluster:
[escalation-4-blogposting-schema-completeness] [escalation-2-og-twitter-meta-unverifiable] [escalation-review-2-unknown-schema] [structured-data-privacy-policy-webpage-schema]

Business Impact Mechanism:
For a consultancy that publishes thought-leadership content, article rich results and social sharing previews are the primary organic amplification mechanisms. A shared article link that renders as a plain URL rather than a rich card with image and headline receives meaningfully less engagement. The fix is a frontmatter field addition and a JSON-LD correction — no architectural changes required.
slides.notes: This cluster is lower urgency than the consent, analytics, and conversion clusters — but it is directly relevant to the firm's content marketing strategy. If WKTW publishes articles to attract organic traffic, those articles should be eligible for rich results and should render correctly when shared. The JSON-LD unknown type finding is particularly important: a misspelled @type causes Google to silently discard the entire structured data block. The investment in writing the JSON-LD produces zero search benefit if the type is unrecognized. Finding IDs: [escalation-4-blogposting-schema-completeness], [escalation-2-og-twitter-meta-unverifiable], [escalation-review-2-unknown-schema], [structured-data-privacy-policy-webpage-schema].

slides.title: Root Cause Cluster 10 — CSS Parse Budget Disproportionate
slides.content:
2 Findings | Medium Severity | Mobile Rendering Performance

The Problem in Plain Language:
The site's CSS parses to 1.53MB uncompressed — a figure that is disproportionate to the site's content complexity. The compressed transfer size is 106KB (reasonable), but the browser must parse and build the CSSOM from the full uncompressed ruleset before it can render anything. On low-end Android devices, this parse work adds measurable delay before first paint.

Important Context:
The 1.53MB figure may be partially a measurement artifact — the audit tooling may be aggregating @font-face declarations, inline style blocks, and data URIs into the CSS transfer figure. The actual stylesheet may be closer to the 7KB reported by the stylesheet count finding. This requires verification before remediation.

If the Figure Is Real:
For a site built with Astro, a 1.53MB CSS parse budget suggests either: (a) a CSS framework (Tailwind, Bootstrap) with incomplete purging of unused utility classes, or (b) a shared stylesheet carrying rules for page types that are never rendered on a given route. The fix is enabling unused-rule elimination at build time and splitting critical-path CSS from deferred CSS.

If the Figure Is a Measurement Artifact:
The measurement pipeline itself needs correction before any CSS remediation is attempted. Acting on a false measurement would waste engineering effort.

Findings in This Cluster:
[resource-loading-css-parse-budget-disproportionate] [resource-loading-css-coverage-measurement-artifact]

Business Impact Mechanism:
On Snapdragon 4xx-class devices — a significant share of global mobile traffic — parsing 1.53MB of CSS rules adds 200–600ms of render-blocking delay before first paint. LCP is directly gated on CSSOM readiness. If the figure is real, this is a measurable Core Web Vitals impact on the mobile audience.
slides.notes: This cluster requires a verification step before remediation. The measurement artifact finding is important context — do not recommend CSS purging or splitting until the measurement is confirmed as accurate. The first action is to run a deterministic CSS coverage audit with proper timing gates. If the 1.53MB figure is confirmed, the remediation path is clear (Tailwind purge configuration or CSS splitting). If it is a measurement artifact, the finding closes as a tooling calibration issue. Finding IDs: [resource-loading-css-parse-budget-disproportionate], [resource-loading-css-coverage-measurement-artifact], [content-to-code-ratio-css-heavy].

slides.title: KPI Impact Analysis — How These Findings Affect Your Business Goals
slides.content:
Primary KPIs: conversion_rate | bounce_rate | contact_form

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
KPI: contact_form (Primary Conversion)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Mechanism 1 — The form cannot be measured
Contact form submissions are not tracked. The conversion rate KPI exists as a business goal but not as a data point. There is no numerator (form completions) and the denominator (total sessions) is suppressed by ad blocker interception of the Plausible script. The KPI is currently unmeasurable.
→ Fix: Three-layer measurement stack (proxied Plausible + GTM dataLayer + server-side submission ping)

Mechanism 2 — The form is the only conversion surface
Every page that is not /contact/ terminates without a conversion mechanism. Visitors who complete service pages, proof content, or articles must independently navigate to /contact/ to convert. Each page-to-page transition is an exit opportunity.
→ Fix: Inline conversion scaffolding on article and service templates; above-fold CTA on homepage

Mechanism 3 — The form has a structural vulnerability
Full-page POST model discards all field values on submission failure. A prospect who composed a detailed inquiry and encounters a network error loses everything and must restart.
→ Fix: Fetch-based submission with field state preservation and inline error handling

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
KPI: bounce_rate
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Mechanism 1 — Touch target failures cause mis-taps
16+ undersized touch targets on every page. Mis-taps on navigation links cause accidental page exits or failed interactions that feel unresponsive. Both outcomes increase the probability of immediate page exit on mobile.
→ Fix: CSS padding and ::before pseudo-element tap-target expanders — no visual redesign required

Mechanism 2 — No above-fold CTA on homepage
Visitors arriving with commercial intent find no visible signal that contact is possible from the homepage. Without a CTA anchor, intent-matched visitors have no on-page action to take.
→ Fix: Hero-section CTA + persistent navigation contact link

Mechanism 3 — Consent banner may consume viewport on mobile
If the consent banner rendering bug is fixed, a banner with 3 interactive elements may consume significant viewport on 393×660px screens, obscuring the value proposition and increasing early exits.
→ Fix: Compact mobile banner layout capped at 40vh

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
KPI: conversion_rate (Composite)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Mechanism — The conversion rate cannot be calculated
Conversion rate = form completions ÷ total sessions. Both variables are currently unreliable: form completions are untracked, and total sessions are suppressed by ad blocker interception. The conversion rate KPI cannot be reported, benchmarked, or optimized until the measurement infrastructure is in place.
→ Fix: Measurement infrastructure is the prerequisite for all conversion rate optimization
slides.notes: This slide is the business case slide. The framing is mechanism-first: explain HOW each finding affects each KPI, not what the percentage impact is. The most important message is that the conversion rate KPI is currently unmeasurable — this is not a performance problem, it is a measurement problem. You cannot optimize what you cannot measure. The three mechanisms for contact_form are ordered by urgency: measurement first (you need to be able to count conversions before you can optimize them), then conversion architecture (more surfaces = more opportunities), then form resilience (protect the conversions you do get). Do NOT invent percentage improvements, dollar amounts, or specific conversion rate estimates. The mechanism explanations are sufficient and defensible.

slides.title: Recommended Roadmap — 30 / 60 / 90 Day Priority Actions
slides.content:
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
DAYS 1–30: Stop the Bleeding — Legal & Measurement
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Priority: Eliminate active legal exposure and establish measurement baseline

① Fix the consent banner rendering bug [CRITICAL — GDPR/CCPA]
The consent system exists. Make it work. This is the prerequisite for all consent-gated analytics.
Findings resolved: [privacy-consent-banner-absent], [consent-banner-not-rendering], [ghost-2-consent-banner-hidden-interactive]

② Fix the consent accept button contrast (1.00:1 → ≥4.5:1) [CRITICAL — WCAG]
The accept button text is invisible. This is a single CSS token change.
Findings resolved: [det-wcag-insufficient-contrast-ratio-https-weknowthewhy-com] (consent component)

③ Deploy Content Security Policy [HIGH — GDPR/Security]
Add CSP header via netlify.toml. Start with report-only mode, graduate to enforced.
Findings resolved: [prescan-escalation-csp-confirmed-absent], [escalation-resolution-csp], [det-security-headers-https-weknowthewhy-com-about]

④ Configure cache headers for static assets [HIGH — Performance]
One netlify.toml change. Content-hashed assets → max-age=31536000, immutable.
Findings resolved: [server-4-cache-control-revalidate-every-request], [server-transport-cdn-cache-status-missing]

⑤ Add contact form submission tracking [CRITICAL — Measurement]
Server-side Netlify Function ping + Plausible custom event. Establishes the conversion rate numerator.
Findings resolved: [analytics-no-ga4-no-conversion-tracking], [analytics-plausible-single-point-failure-conversion-blindspot]

⑥ Fix the unlabeled form field on /contact/ [HIGH — WCAG/ADA]
Likely a single aria-hidden attribute on the Netlify hidden input. Verify and apply.
Findings resolved: [a11y-1-form-missing-label], [form-1-missing-label]

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
DAYS 31–60: Build the Conversion Engine
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Priority: Create conversion paths across the site; fix remaining WCAG exposure

⑦ Add above-fold CTA to homepage [HIGH — Conversion]
Hero-section CTA button + persistent navigation contact link.
Findings resolved: [conv-contact-cta-path-missing], [ux-conversion-01 (CTA below fold)]

⑧ Add conversion scaffold to article template [HIGH — Conversion]
End-of-article inline contact form or CTA + sticky bottom-bar. Server-rendered in template.
Findings resolved: [conv-ux-001], [ux-conversion-no-form-on-page], [revenue-no-service-page-link-in-article]

⑨ Fix touch targets across all pages [HIGH — WCAG/Mobile]
CSS padding + ::before pseudo-element expanders on navigation, footer, and consent banner.
Findings resolved: [a11y-2-touch-targets-undersized], [touch-targets-ux-impact], [mobile-privacy-policy-link-critically-undersized], [conv-ux-001-touch-target-form-suppression], [mob-touch-001]

⑩ Fix design system contrast tokens [MEDIUM — WCAG]
Update color tokens for navigation links (1.06:1 → ≥4.5:1) and metadata spans (1.62:1 → ≥4.5:1). Add text-decoration: underline to inline links.
Findings resolved: [a11y-compound-contrast-color-indicator-failure], [det-wcag-color-as-sole-indicator-https-weknowthewhy-com-about]

⑪ Add accessible names to icon-only links and footer button [MEDIUM — WCAG]
aria-label on 9 icon links + footer button. Single template edit.
Findings resolved: [a11y-icon-links-empty-accessible-name-svg], [interactive-footer-button-purpose-unclear], [det-wcag-missing-name-role-value-https-weknowthewhy-com-contact]

⑫ Fix form error handling — fetch-based submission [MEDIUM — Conversion]
Replace full-page POST with fetch handler. Add field state preservation and inline error messages.
Findings resolved: [conversion-no-error-recovery-evidence]

⑬ Scope JetBrains Mono to CodeBlock component [MEDIUM — Performance]
Move @font-face declaration from global CSS to CodeBlock scoped styles.
Findings resolved: [js-2-jetbrains-mono-font-loaded-unnecessarily], [font-face-overload-3-families]

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
DAYS 61–90: Optimize & Harden
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Priority: Search visibility, trust signals, measurement refinement, regression prevention

⑭ Fix JSON-LD structured data [MEDIUM — SEO]
Correct unknown @type, add absolute URLs to logo/image properties, add image to BlogPosting.
Findings resolved: [escalation-review-2-unknown-schema], [escalation-4-blogposting-schema-completeness]

⑮ Verify and complete Open Graph / Twitter Card metadata [MEDIUM — SEO]
Ensure og:image, og:description, twitter:card present on all page types.
Findings resolved: [escalation-2-og-twitter-meta-unverifiable]

⑯ Add trust signals to /proof/ and service pages [MEDIUM — Conversion]
Testimonials, client logos, or case study result metrics at conversion decision points.
Findings resolved: [trust-signals-absent-at-conversion-points], [revenue-no-social-proof-above-fold]

⑰ Add pricing/engagement model signals to service pages [MEDIUM — Conversion]
Scope indicators and engagement model framing to reduce inquiry friction.
Findings resolved: [ux-revenue-no-pricing-signals]

⑱ Verify and fix CSS parse budget [MEDIUM — Performance]
Run deterministic CSS coverage audit. If 1.53MB confirmed, enable Tailwind purge or CSS splitting.
Findings resolved: [resource-loading-css-parse-budget-disproportionate], [resource-loading-css-coverage-measurement-artifact]

⑲ Add Plausible first-party proxy [MEDIUM — Measurement]
Netlify rewrite rule to proxy plausible.io through the site's own domain.
Findings resolved: [ux-analytics-client-side-only-data-loss]

⑳ Add tel: link and click-to-call affordance [LOW — Mobile Conversion]
tel: link in navigation + mobile sticky call bar + Organization schema telephone property.
Findings resolved: [mobile-mailto-not-tel-friendly], [no-click-to-call-mobile-contact-friction]
slides.notes: The roadmap is ordered by: (1) legal exposure elimination, (2) measurement establishment, (3) conversion architecture, (4) optimization. This ordering is deliberate — you cannot optimize what you cannot measure, and you should not be accumulating legal exposure while building conversion features. The 30-day items are all either configuration changes (netlify.toml), single-attribute fixes (aria-hidden, aria-label), or CSS token changes. None require architectural work. The 60-day items are template-level changes — they require development effort but are scoped to specific components. The 90-day items are optimization and hardening — they improve an already-functional system. Emphasize that items ①–⑥ can be completed in parallel by a single developer in the first two weeks. The consent banner fix (①) is the highest-leverage single action in the entire audit.

slides.title: What's Working — Preserve These Strengths
slides.content:
The audit identified genuine architectural strengths that should be protected as the site evolves.

✓ Server Performance: Exceptional
• TTFB 121ms — top cohort for static sites
• HTTP/2 + TLS 1.3 — optimal transport layer
• No redirect chains, no mixed content
• These are structural outcomes of the Netlify + Astro stack — preserve them

✓ Interaction Responsiveness: Excellent
• INP 24ms — well within the 'Good' threshold (<200ms)
• 29KB total JavaScript — minimal parse and execution overhead
• Single third-party script (Plausible) — no third-party render blocking
• Zero JS-injected DOM elements — raw and rendered HTML are identical

✓ Visual Stability: Perfect
• CLS 0.000 — no layout shifts detected on any tested page
• Font loading correctly configured — self-hosted, font-display: swap, preloads correct
• DOM is lean and well-structured — 142–146 elements, semantic HTML

✓ Backwards Compatibility: Strong
• Astro static HTML with minimal JS — strong posture for legacy device support
• Navigation fully present in raw HTML — no JS-dependent navigation
• Heading hierarchy correct on most pages — single h1, no skipped levels

✓ Content Architecture: Clean Foundation
• No ghost markup, no duplicate landmarks, no orphaned assets
• No duplicate event listeners or dead interaction zones detected
• Internal link depth acceptable — key pages within 1–2 clicks

The Implication:
The performance foundation is genuinely strong. The risks identified in this audit are concentrated in compliance, measurement, and conversion architecture — not in the core technical stack. Remediation does not require rebuilding the site.
slides.notes: This slide is important for tone and credibility. A ruthless audit that only identifies problems reads as adversarial. Acknowledging genuine strengths — especially when they are real and significant — builds trust and demonstrates that the audit is objective. The 24ms INP and 121ms TTFB are genuinely excellent numbers. The zero CLS is a real achievement. The clean DOM architecture is a sign of careful development. These should be celebrated and protected. The closing implication is the key message: the site does not need to be rebuilt. The problems are specific and addressable. This sets up the roadmap as achievable rather than overwhelming.

slides.title: Next Steps — Implementation Support & Engagement Continuation
slides.content:
Three Paths Forward

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Path 1 — Audit Delivery Only
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
You receive the full technical audit report with all 92 findings, root cause analysis, and implementation-ready code examples. Your development team implements the roadmap using the audit as a specification.

Best for: Teams with in-house development capacity who need a clear, prioritized specification.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Path 2 — Audit + Implementation Sprint
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
We implement the 30-day priority items alongside your team: consent banner fix, CSP deployment, cache header configuration, form tracking, and WCAG critical fixes. Deliverable: a site that is legally compliant and measurable within 30 days.

Best for: Teams who want the highest-priority items resolved quickly without internal resource allocation.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Path 3 — Full Remediation Partnership
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
We implement the full 90-day roadmap, establish CI/CD regression gates, and deliver a post-remediation audit to verify all findings are resolved. Includes measurement infrastructure setup, conversion architecture implementation, and structured data completion.

Best for: Teams who want the full audit translated into a production-ready implementation with verification.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Immediate Actions (Before Any Engagement Decision)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
These items can be actioned today, regardless of path chosen:

1. Investigate the consent banner rendering condition — check browser console for JS errors on page load
2. Add Cache-Control headers to netlify.toml — one configuration change, zero risk
3. Add aria-hidden to the Netlify hidden form field — one attribute, eliminates a WCAG Level A violation
4. Update the consent accept button color token — one CSS variable change, eliminates the 1.00:1 contrast failure

Contact: [Auditor contact information]
slides.notes: The three-path structure gives the client agency without overwhelming them with options. Path 1 is the minimum viable engagement — the audit is the deliverable. Path 2 is the highest-urgency option — it addresses legal exposure and measurement in 30 days. Path 3 is the full partnership. The 'Immediate Actions' block is important: it gives the client something they can do today, regardless of which path they choose. This demonstrates good faith and reduces the 'analysis paralysis' risk where a client receives a large audit and does nothing while deciding what to do. The four immediate actions are all genuinely low-risk, high-impact items that require no architectural decisions. Customize the contact information and engagement model details before presenting.

slides.content:
Cluster 1 — Broken Consent System (8 findings)
[privacy-consent-banner-absent] [consent-banner-not-rendering] [privacy-2-pre-consent-plausible-fires] [ghost-2-consent-banner-hidden-interactive] [mobile-privacy-policy-link-critically-undersized] [interactive-footer-button-purpose-unclear] [ux-mobile-consent-banner-viewport-impact] [prescan-escalation-console-errors]

[ux-conversion-no-form-on-page] [ux-conversion-01] [revenue-no-service-page-link-in-article] [ux-conversion-cta-clarity-competing-actions] [trust-signals-absent-at-conversion-points] [revenue-no-social-proof-above-fold] [ux-revenue-no-pricing-signals] [ux-conversion-01 (CTA below fold)] [prescan-override-1-contact-form-severity]

slides.notes: This appendix slide is a reference document for the technical team. It maps every finding ID to its root cause cluster, enabling developers to cross-reference the presentation with the full technical report. The passing findings list is included to demonstrate audit completeness — the audit checked these items and they passed, which is meaningful signal. In the presentation, this slide is typically not presented verbally — it is left as a reference for the client to consult when reviewing the full report. Consider providing this as a separate handout or as the final page of the written report.
